content: Lipid management is cornerstone of ASCVD prevention and treatment. LDL cholesterol is primary target—causally linked to atherosclerosis (genetic, epidemiologic, imaging, RCT evidence). Risk stratification guides intensity: very high risk (clinical ASCVD, multiple major events, or 1 high-risk event plus multiple conditions)—LDL goal <70 mg/dL; high risk (diabetes age 40-75, severe CKD, LDL ≥190, 10-year ASCVD risk ≥20%)—goal <100 mg/dL; moderate risk (diabetes age 40-75 without complications, 10-year risk 7.5-20%)—goal <130 mg/dL. Non-HDL cholesterol (total minus HDL) and apolipoprotein B (apoB, particle number) are secondary targets, especially with hypertriglyceridemia. Lp(a) measured once in lifetime—elevated (>50 mg/dL or >125 nmol/L) is independent risk factor. Triglycerides: nonfasting acceptable for screening; if ≥175 mg/dL, consider lifestyle, address secondary causes.

Lipid-lowering therapy algorithm: First-line high-intensity statin for all with clinical ASCVD or high risk. If LDL remains above threshold on maximally tolerated statin, add ezetimibe (inhibits intestinal cholesterol absorption—IMPROVE-IT showed additional CV benefit). If still above threshold, add PCSK9 inhibitor (alirocumab, evolocumab—monoclonal antibodies that increase LDL receptor recycling; FOURIER and ODYSSEY OUTCOMES showed ~15% relative risk reduction per 39 mg/dL LDL reduction). Inclisiran (siRNA targeting PCSK9) allows twice-yearly dosing after initial doses. Bempedoic acid (ATP citrate lyase inhibitor) oral alternative; CLEAR OUTCOMES showed CV benefit but increased gout/amputation risk. Bile acid sequestrants rarely used (GI side effects). Fibrates for severe hypertriglyceridemia (>500 mg/dL to prevent pancreatitis); no consistent CV benefit when added to statins (ACCORD-LIPID). Icosapent ethyl (pure EPA) reduced CV events in REDUCE-IT trial (high-risk patients with TG 135-499 on statins).

Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH): autosomal dominant disorder (LDLR, APOB, PCSK9 mutations) causing severely elevated LDL from birth. Heterozygous FH (1:200-250 prevalence): untreated LDL 190-450 mg/dL, premature ASCVD (men <55, women <60). Homozygous FH (1:160,000-300,000): LDL >450-1000 mg/dL, childhood ASCVD, tendon xanthomas, corneal arcus. Diagnosis: Dutch Lipid Clinic Network criteria (clinical history, physical exam, LDL level, family history, genetic testing). Cascade screening of first-degree relatives essential. Treatment: high-intensity statin from diagnosis (children as young as 8-10 years), often need combination therapy (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitor). Homozygous FH may require lomitapide (MTP inhibitor), evinacumab (angiopoietin-like 3 inhibitor), or LDL apheresis (extracorporeal LDL removal every 1-2 weeks). New therapies in development: gene therapy, mRNA-based approaches. Early diagnosis and aggressive treatment critical—lifetime exposure to high LDL drives ASCVD risk.
